Visitor.title: Visitor
Visitor.description: Represents a person who visits the NEU Library, storing their identification details and status.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Visitor entity. This can be the student/employee ID or a unique identifier from an external authentication system.

name.description: The full name of the visitor.

college.description: The college or office the visitor is affiliated with.

classification.description: The category of the visitor: Student, Staff, or Administrator.

role.description: The authorization level of the user: 'user' or 'admin'.

isBlocked.description: A flag indicating whether the visitor is currently blocked from accessing the library.

createdAt.description: The timestamp when this visitor record was first created.

updatedAt.description: The timestamp when this visitor record was last updated.

VisitLog.title: VisitLog
VisitLog.description: Records details of a single visit made by a person to the NEU Library.

id.description: Unique identifier for the VisitLog entity.

visitorId.description: Reference to the Visitor who made this log entry. (Relationship: Visitor 1:N VisitLog)

timestamp.description: The date and time when the visit was logged.

purpose.description: The stated purpose of the visitor's visit to the library. E.g., Reading Books, Thesis Research, Computer Usage, Doing Assignments.

classification.description: The classification of the visitor at the time of entry.

definition.description: Stores individual visitor profiles. The `visitorId` can be a Firebase UID (for Google sign-in) or an external ID (for 'Tap ID'). This document is the source of truth for `isBlocked` and contains all necessary visitor identification details. Includes `isBlocked` for direct authorization checks by rules/application logic.

params.name: visitorId
params.description: Unique identifier for the Visitor, corresponding to either a Firebase Authentication UID or an external ID from the 'Tap ID' system.

definition.description: Records individual visit entries for a specific visitor. The `visitorId` is denormalized into the document path for clear ownership and efficient rule evaluation. The document also contains the `visitorId` field as per the schema, ensuring authorization independence and simplifying queries for a specific visitor's logs.

params.name: visitorId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent Visitor.

params.name: visitLogId
params.description: Unique identifier for this specific visit log entry.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure is designed to support both authenticated (Google Sign-in) and unauthenticated ('Tap ID') visitor flows while adhering strictly to the core design principles, especially Authorization Independence and QAPs.The primary collection, `/visitors/{visitorId}`, stores individual visitor profiles. The `visitorId` document ID here is critical: for Google-authenticated users, it will be their `request.auth.uid`; for 'Tap ID' users, it will be the unique external ID provided. This collection serves as the source of truth for visitor details, including the `isBlocked` status.

To ensure Authorization Independence for `VisitLog` entries, a subcollection `/visitors/{visitorId}/visitLogs/{visitLogId}` is used. This structure leverages hierarchical paths to naturally link visit logs to their respective visitors. Importantly, the `VisitLog` document *itself* will denormalize the `visitorId` field (as per the entity schema). This denormalization means that security rules for `VisitLog` can directly verify `request.auth.uid == resource.data.visitorId` (for authenticated users) or rely on a trusted Cloud Function for 'Tap ID' scenarios, without needing to perform a `get()` operation on the parent `Visitor` document. This directly addresses the Authorization Independence principle.

Regarding QAPs (Rules are not Filters):
1.  **Visitor Profile Access:** Authenticated users can query their own `Visitor` document by path (`/visitors/$(request.auth.uid)`). Administrators would have broader access to the `/visitors` collection.
2.  **Visit Log Access:** Authenticated users can list their own visit logs efficiently by querying their specific subcollection (`/visitors/$(request.auth.uid)/visitLogs`). This is a direct query with no filtering required by security rules, thus supporting QAPs. For administrative purposes, listing all visit logs across all visitors can be achieved using a performant Collection Group Query on `visitLogs`, which is also QAP-friendly. The 'Tap ID' flow, requiring a Cloud Function to write logs, bypasses client-side rules for unauthenticated users, further simplifying rule logic for QAPs.

The `isBlocked` flag is stored directly in the `Visitor` document. The application logic (both client-side for Google users and Cloud Function for 'Tap ID' users) will perform the `isBlocked` check *before* attempting to create a `VisitLog` or granting further access, rather than relying on complex `get()` calls within Firestore security rules for every `VisitLog` write operation. This delegates the critical, state-dependent authorization check to the application layer, simplifying rules to primarily enforce data ownership and structural integrity.
